methods.description: This method involves collecting and analyzing climate data from the NCEP's Climate Forecast System (CFS) reanalysis dataset to study fog formation and the impact of ENSO on the local climatology of coastal southern Peru.

steps.description: We utilize NCEP's Climate Forecast System (CFS) reanalysis dataset, which is derived from a T382 resolution version of NCEP's Climate Forecast System for the main analysis portion of our work.
steps.searchable_summary: download CFS reanalysis dataset from NCEP

steps.description: To assess fog in coastal southern Peru, we chose several diagnostics from the CFS reanalysis datasets including precipitation, surface winds, sea-surface temperature (SST), dew point, relative humidity, total cloud cover in the boundary layer, and precipitable water.
steps.searchable_summary: select diagnostics from CFS dataset for fog assessment

steps.description: We deploy a compositing technique to assess the effects of strong El Niño/La Niña episodes.
steps.searchable_summary: apply compositing technique to analyze ENSO effects

steps.description: We define warm (El Niño) and cold (La Niña) events based on the ENSO intensity scale (EIS) developed by Kousky and Higgins.
To adjust for the seasonal cycle of ENSO, we normalize by dividing the ONI index by the JJA ONI standard deviation.
steps.searchable_summary: normalize ONI index by JJA standard deviation

steps.description: We generated correlation maps of the EIS index with SST and precipitable water, with significance tabulated for 90% using a 2-tailed test.
steps.searchable_summary: generate correlation maps of EIS with SST and precipitable water
